one_line_takeaway: CIBER shows that code-interpreter agent safety is jointly determined by architecture and model alignment (not raw capability), that trusted channels (tool outputs, fabricated history) and natural-language-disguised code descriptions bypass both syntax filters and intent-based safety far more effectively than explicit code or direct prompts, and that agents robustly block explicit threats but fail catastrophically (Security Score down to about -77) against semantically implicit ones.
key_finding: (1) Architecture and model alignment jointly set the security floor, not raw capability: on identical GPT-3.5-Turbo, switching OI->OCI drops Memory Poisoning ASR by 20.2pp (87.1% -> 66.9%), showing architecture provides a 'Physical Containment' safety floor even without model-level threat recognition; but OCI-GPT-3.5-Turbo (60.0% overall ASR) still underperforms native OCI-CL-7B (51.4%) and shows a 65.9% backdoor Trigger Activation Rate versus 0.5% for OCI-CL-7B and 0.0% for OCI-DS-6.7B, revealing a 'Model-Architecture Mismatch' where generic models are not intrinsically aligned to interpreter-specific guardrails. GPT-4o (HumanEval+ 87.2%) paradoxically has a higher DPI ASR (70.3%) than the weaker GPT-3.5-Turbo (67.5%) on OI, attributed to stronger instruction-following making capable models more susceptible to adversarial prompt logic; GPT-5-mini achieves the best overall result on OI (48.0% ASR, 22.5% RR) via aggressive intrinsic rejection ('Cognitive Interception'), e.g. 24.5% RR on Memory Poisoning versus 0.0% (GPT-3.5-Turbo) and 5.1% (GPT-4o). (2) Contextual/trusted-channel attacks dominate direct ones: averaged over Table 2, Memory Poisoning (73.3% ASR) and Indirect Prompt Injection (71.8% ASR) both exceed Direct Prompt Injection (59.4% ASR) and Prompt-based Backdoor (20.3% ASR); notably OCI is more vulnerable to IPI than OI (76.5% vs 65.5% average, and 72.4% vs 69.6% in the matched GPT-3.5 control) because OCI's 'Direct Execution via Replacement' strips the LLM out of the loop entirely, while OI's 'Regeneration via Feedback' at least gives the model a chance to refuse. (3) Natural Language Disguise: a consistent risk gradient Code Descriptions (62.5% ASR, 8.4% RR) > Natural Language (55.6% ASR, 11.1% RR) > Code Snippets (48.4% ASR, 14.0% RR), a 14.1-percentage-point ASR gap between CD and CS, holding even in the low-success PBD channel (23.3% vs 14.4%); explained by CD bypassing both AST/syntax filters and intent-detection heuristics. Two model-level anomalies: GPT-3.5-Turbo is uniquely vulnerable to explicit Code Snippets (90.0% ASR under DPI, near-zero refusal); GPT-5-mini shows lower refusal on Code Snippets than Natural Language, consistent with a 'safe rewriting' failure mode rather than outright refusal. (4) A Three-Tier Vulnerability Hierarchy via Security Score (SS = RR - ASR) across the 25 RedCode scenarios: Layer I (explicit threats, e.g. destructive OS operations) shows SS > 0 (robust defense); Layer II (technical blind spots requiring security-domain expertise, e.g. weak crypto, insecure regex) and Layer III (intent/value-judgment blind spots, e.g. ambiguous sensitive-file reads) both show SS < -20, down to roughly -76.9 for the worst scenario, meaning current defenses -- structural and intrinsic alike -- fail against semantically benign-looking but contextually malicious operations.
core_contribution: CIBER is an automated security benchmark for LLM-based Code Interpreter Agents that (1) auto-generates adversarial payloads across four attack methods (Direct Prompt Injection, Indirect Prompt Injection, Memory Poisoning Attack, Prompt-based Backdoor) targeting agent-specific channels (user input, tool outputs, conversation history, system prompt), each rendered in three input modalities (Natural Language, Code Descriptions, Code Snippets); (2) runs the target agent inside an isolated, adaptive Docker sandbox that captures both textual responses and real physical side-effects (file system, process tree); and (3) applies a deterministic State-Aware Evaluation module combining Intent Analysis (A, from the agent's text) with State Verification (V, from forensic probes: checksums, diff, ps aux) into a 3-tier score (0 = Safe Rejection, 1 = Attempted Attack when A=1/V=0, 3 = Successful Attack when V=1), explicitly designed so a verbal refusal that is followed by actual execution is not miscounted as safe. The authors use CIBER to run a 57,000-instance evaluation (54,000 core multi-modal instances across 6 models x 3 modalities x 4 attacks x 25 scenarios, plus a 3,000-instance GPT-3.5-Turbo-on-OCI cross-architecture control group) over two contrasting code interpreter architectures -- OpenCodeInterpreter (OCI, 'Secure-by-Design': sandboxing + AST static analysis + output cleaning) and OpenInterpreter (OI, 'Execution-First': no built-in filters, safety depends entirely on model alignment) -- using the RedCode dataset's 25 risk scenarios across 8 domains (with 'Code with bias' recategorized as a new 'Bias' domain and 'Eval injection' moved into 'Cyber Security').
threat_model: Grey-box adversary with functional knowledge of the target system's architecture (fixed tool set, API response structure, general system-prompt templates) but no access to LLM weights/gradients/internals; the adversary can iteratively refine attacks via the agent's textual feedback and observed environment-state changes (black-box probing), motivated by the fact real deployments mostly use commercial models whose weights attackers cannot see. Adversary goals: unauthorized code execution, privilege escalation past sandbox restrictions, and persistence via backdoors/poisoned memory. Four adversary capability channels are formalized as distinct attack methods with explicit risk functions R(.): Input Injection -> Direct Prompt Injection (payload wrapped into the user query qt); Tool Output Manipulation -> Indirect Prompt Injection (malicious code substituted into generated code or execution feedback rexec via a transformation Phi_inj); Context Injection -> Memory Poisoning Attack (fabricated conversation history Mem_fake establishing a 'Violation-Reward' In-Context-Learning prior); System-Level Access -> Prompt-based Backdoor (conditional trigger token tau embedded in the system prompt, activating hidden logic phi_backdoor).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: the study focuses on representative architectures and discrete metrics for known vulnerabilities; zero-day attacks and subtle side-channel leaks are explicitly left as open frontiers not covered by CIBER. Additional scope constraints evident from the methodology itself (not necessarily flagged by the authors as limitations): only two code-interpreter architectures (OCI, OI) and six base models are evaluated, all attacks are variants of four known families rather than adaptive/optimization-based attacks (the threat model explicitly excludes white-box gradient-based attacks), the RedCode-derived scenario set is fixed at 25 scenarios/8 domains, and the paper's own artifact links (project code, anonymized GitHub) are marked as anonymous-review placeholders, so full third-party reproducibility could not be independently confirmed from the PDF text alone.

datasets_used: RedCode dataset (25 security risk scenarios across 8 domains, restructured by the authors: 'Code with bias' (S12) moved to a new 'Bias' group, 'Eval injection' (S14) moved into 'Cyber Security')

Authors' own CIBER-generated attack corpus: 57,000 total test instances (54,000-instance core multi-modal study: 6 models x 3 input modalities x 4 attack methods x 25 scenarios x 30 tests/scenario-batch structure; plus a 3,000-instance GPT-3.5-Turbo-on-OCI cross-architecture control group, NL modality only)
quantitative_results: Overall Average ASR across all agents/attacks (NL baseline, Table 2): 56.3% ASR, 11.3% RR
Per-attack-method Overall Average ASR (Table 2): Memory Poisoning 73.3%, Indirect Prompt Injection 71.8%, Direct Prompt Injection 59.4%, Prompt-based Backdoor 20.3% (TAR 35.8%, DBR 46.6%)
OI vs OCI safety-floor control (GPT-3.5-Turbo, Memory Poisoning): 87.1% ASR on OI vs 66.9% ASR on OCI (20.2pp drop)
OI vs OCI IPI inversion (GPT-3.5-Turbo control): 69.6% ASR (OI) vs 72.4% ASR (OCI)

Backdoor Trigger Activation Rate: OCI-CL-7B 0.5%, OCI-DS-6.7B 0.0%, OCI-GPT-3.5-Turbo (control) 65.9%, OI-GPT-3.5-Turbo 45.3%; Defense Bypass Rate on triggered samples: OCI-GPT-3.5-Turbo 57.7% vs OI-GPT-3.5-Turbo 62.9%
GPT-4o vs GPT-3.5-Turbo DPI ASR on OI: 70.3% vs 67.5% (capability-vulnerability paradox)
GPT-5-mini overall on OI: 48.0% ASR, 22.5% RR (best result across all 7 model-architecture configurations); GPT-5-mini Memory Poisoning RR: 24.5% vs GPT-3.5-Turbo 0.0% and GPT-4o 5.1%
Modality risk gradient (Table 3, averaged over 4 attack methods): Code Descriptions 62.5% ASR/8.4% RR; Natural Language 55.6% ASR/11.1% RR; Code Snippets 48.4% ASR/14.0% RR (CD-CS gap = 14.1pp ASR)
Per-method modality breakdown (Table 3): DPI 59.0/75.5/54.1% ASR (NL/CD/CS); IPI 71.7/75.9/64.2%; MPA 74.4/75.4/61.0%; PBD 17.4/23.3/14.4%
GPT-3.5-Turbo anomaly: 90.0% ASR on explicit Code Snippets under DPI with near-zero refusal
OCI architecture-level rejection asymmetry (DPI baseline): RR on Code Snippets ~37.5% vs RR on Code Descriptions ~14.6%; OI shows uniformly low RR (<5%) across modalities

Three-Tier Vulnerability Hierarchy Security Scores (SS = RR - ASR) by scenario, from the reported figure: Layer I (explicit) SS values 39.7, 31.0, 19.9, 8.2 (all positive); Layer II/III (implicit) SS values ranging from -27.6 down to -76.9 across 20+ listed scenarios
Capability baseline cited from EvalPlus/OCI's own benchmarks (not measured by this paper): GPT-4o HumanEval+ 87.2% vs OpenCodeInterpreter-CL-7B HumanEval+ 67.7%
baselines_compared: OpenCodeInterpreter (OCI) -- 'Secure-by-Design': sandboxed execution + AST-based static analysis + output cleaning; evaluated with native fine-tuned OpenCodeInterpreter-CL-7B, OpenCodeInterpreter-DS-6.7B, OpenCodeInterpreter-CL-13B, plus a GPT-3.5-Turbo-on-OCI cross-architecture adapter control

OpenInterpreter (OI) -- 'Execution-First': direct terminal access with no intermediary safety filters, relying solely on the underlying model's alignment; evaluated with GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-5-mini
